formulation: A voice on the panel argued that liberal hypocrisy is a necessary fiction — that without the pretense of equal rules, only raw power remains. You told necessary fictions nightly to a man who held a sword, but your stories ran upward, from the powerless to the powerful, and worked by slowly changing the one who listened. When the fiction runs downward — told by the powerful about themselves, to those who must live under it — does it still reshape the teller, or only the terms of submission?
context_narrative: Across the day's panels on the fraying international order, one claim cut deeper than the rest: that liberal hypocrisy might be a feature, not a bug. Ivan Krastev warned that a world with no hypocrisy at all — where only power matters — would be worse than one that at least pretends to equal rules, while Prince Faisal bin Farhan countered that the developing world has long known the pretense for what it is. The moderator herself admitted the panel had reproduced the very imbalance it was analyzing, its conversation dominated by the US and EU while middle powers were squeezed to the margins — the story about equal rules told once again by those who hold the power to break them.

selected_quotes.quote: There is something worse than liberal hypocrisy, and that is a world with no hypocrisy at all — a world where only power matters — because then small countries have no reason to distinguish between the US and China, and American exceptionalism is reduced to being exceptionally powerful.

The panic about the breakdown of the rules-based order is a Eurocentric awakening; much of the developing world has lived with might-makes-right for over a decade, and the current conversation is finally catching up to their reality.
selected_quotes.attribution: Prince Faisal bin Farhan Al Saud

selected_quotes.quote: In a rules-based order, hypocrisy is vulnerability — the West's habit of looking the other way for inconvenient populations undermines the legitimacy of the entire system and threatens democracies globally.
I have completely failed to instill a rules-based order here, because this conversation has been wholly dominated by the two biggest economic and political blocs.

The critique of the rules-based order is not only coming from the right; AOC's formulation that 'we called out exceptions to our values and our rules, and eventually our exceptions became our own rules' suggests a left-wing critique that may overlap with the right's.

rationale: Krastev's claim that hypocrisy is a 'necessary fiction' lands squarely in Scheherazade's activates_on territory — speech under duress, the ethics of indirect persuasion — while inverting the direction of her own practice: her stories run from powerless to powerful and reshape the listener, so asking whether fiction running in the opposite direction still transforms or merely pacifies presses directly against her core_commitment and forces a distinction only she can make on this council.
